A large upper level low pressure system moved from northwest Kansas into south central Nebraska during the late afternoon and evening of June 20.  Temperatures and dew point temperatures ahead of the low climbed to around 90 and lower 70s respectively near and south of a warm front that extended east northeast from low pressure in southwest Nebraska.  Strong levels of wind shear combined with the high instability to produce several spiral bands of embedded supercell thunderstorms that produced fairly widespread wind damage and at least 2 tornadoes across the region.

This Saturday evening found South Central Nebraska on the very fringes of an area under a considerable risk of severe thunderstorm development, as a strong warm front focused a pronounced corridor of severe storms from northeast Nebraska into northwest Iowa, including several strong tornadoes in Iowa. Closer to home, only one strong storm managed to develop in far eastern South Central Nebraska early in the evening, affecting portions of York and Polk Counties and producing penny size hail in Shelby. The primary reason that South Central Nebraska was spared significant severe weather during the evening was that most of the area ended up behind a pronounced north-south oriented surface dryline, which focused the most significant instability farther east. In addition, the primary east-west oriented surface warm front that storms developed along stretched mainly north of the area. Despite the lack of severe storm coverage in South Central Nebraska, the mesoscale environment in eastern counties was conducive for severe weather with up to around 3000 J/kg of mixed-layer CAPE, and 50-60 knots of deep-layer wind shear. The northward surge of warm air south of the warm front resulted in record high temperatures across parts of the region, including an 89 degree reading at Central Nebraska Regional Airport in Grand Island.

The combined influence of the leading edge of a strong cold front, along with possible contribution from thunderstorm outflow from generally weak storms in the area brought strong northwesterly wind gusts to parts of South Central Nebraska on this Friday evening, highlighted by a 58 MPH gust at Evelyn Sharp Field in Ord. These strong northerly gusts in the evening capped off a day that also featured strong southerly winds ahead of the invading cold front. Around the time of the 58 MPH gust in Ord, the leading edge of the surface cold front generally bisected the state of Nebraska, extending north-northeast from a 990 millibar low pressure center in southwest Kansas. Wind gusts across the majority of South Central Nebraska were not quite as strong as the cold front passed, with other measured speeds including 51 MPH at Hastings, and 49 MPH in Grand Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 58 mph wind gust was measured at the Ord Airport ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High releases from upstream reservoirs brought increasing flows and flooding along the Missouri River downstream of Gavins Point Dam.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High releases from upstream reservoirs brought steadily increasing flows along the Missouri River.  At Omaha the river climbed to around its 29 foot flood stage late on May 27th, and then continued rising into June.  The flooding in Douglas county along the Missouri closed a park and baseball diamonds on the northwest side of Omaha, and low areas around downtown on the river side of the Levee began flooding when flood gates were installed.  Boating and other recreational areas were also either flooded or closed by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High releases from upstream reservoirs brought increasing flows and flooding along the Missouri River downstream of Gavins Point Dam.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High releases from upstream reservoirs brought steadily increasing flows and flooding along the Missouri River.  The river near Blair climbed to around its 26.5 foot flood stage in the morning of May 26th, and then continued rising into June.  The flooding in Washington county was especially felt in the Fort Calhoun where at least 60 homes or cabins were flooded or cutoff by flood waters.  The high school in Ft. Calhoun was opened as a shelter for flood victims.  In addition, recreation areas along the river were flooded.

An upper level disturbance that lifted across the Rockies and into the Dakotas sent a cold front across eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa during the late evening and overnight hours of June 26th and 27th.  In addition, a warm front extended ahead of the cold front near the Kansas border.  Moderate to high amounts of instability and wind shear allowed several clusters of severe thunderstorms to race across the area both ahead of and along the cold front.   Bowing segments across northeast Nebraska and west central Iowa produced areas of very strong damaging winds.  Slower moving supercell thunderstorms that developed north of the warm front produced a few large hail and strong wind reports in southeast Nebraska along with very heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of up to 4 inches fell in less than 12 hours across parts of the Big Blue River basin near Crete.  The heavy rain caused the Big Blue River near Crete to flood on June 27th.  The river at Crete crested a little under 19 feet during the afternoon of June 27th, flood stage is 18 feet.  The flooding mainly affected farmland adjacent to the river along with a few county roads briefly affected as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell Thunderstorms developed in a short line from southeast Nebraska into southwest Iowa during the late evening of June 19th.  The storms formed near a weak warm front that separated dew point temperatures in the lower 70s south of the boundary with slightly drier conditions to the north.  The storms produced mainly large hail and at least one tornado on the Iowa and Missouri Border north of Tarkio Missouri.   A little later that night another line formed along and a little south of Highway 30 from around Columbus into western Iowa.  These storms produced strong winds, a few large hail reports and a band of heavy rain of 2 to 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated at over 60 mph downed tree limbs several inches in diameter south of Cedar Bluffs and destroyed a large metal building west of town.  Two center pivots in the area were also flipped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm developed in central Cherry county and tracked through northeast Cherry county during the late afternoon hours. Large hail and strong winds gusts were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure system moved from northwest Kansas into south central Nebraska during the late afternoon and evening of June 20.  Temperatures and dew point temperatures ahead of the low climbed to around 90 and lower 70s respectively near and south of a warm front that extended east northeast from low pressure in southwest Nebraska.  Strong levels of wind shear combined with the high instability to produce several spiral bands of embedded supercell thunderstorms that produced fairly widespread wind damage and at least 2 tornadoes across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at around 70 mph downed trees and did farm outbuilding damage from around Wausa to near Bloomfield.  Downed trees also caused some home damage in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level system dropped southeast from the northern Rockies and then split into two pieces, one tracked across the central plains while the other drifted southwest across the southwestern U.S.  Surface low pressure associated with the first piece tracked from northeast Texas across southeast Missouri and then into the lower Great Lakes region.  Arctic air was pulled in behind this system and brought a massive winter storm to a very large part of the U.S.  Freezing rain, heavy snow and blizzard conditions prevailed from the southern plains toward the Great Lakes, while record setting cold temperatures plunged southwest across western Texas, New Mexico and Arizona.||Across eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa the storm initially spread freezing drizzle or light freezing rain, mixed at times with light snow, across much of the area during the early morning hours of Jan. 31.  The precipitation changed over to all snow later on the 31st and continued into the late night hours of Feb. 1st.  As Arctic air spilled in behind the system, dangerously cold wind chill values spread over the region on the 1st and early on the 2nd.   Increasing north to northwest winds on the 1st caused considerable blowing and drifting snow which closed some county roads and created near-blizzard conditions.  The heavier snow from the storm was generally from around Lincoln and David City east and southeast into western Iowa, with most amounts across this area in the 5 to 7 inch range.  Heavier snow totals from the storm in eastern Nebraska included; 6.9 inches in Omaha, 6.6 inches at the NWS in Valley, 6.2 inches in Virginia, and around 6 inches in Rulo, Shubert, Auburn, near Cedar Bluffs and Papillion and around 5 inches at David City, Lincoln and Beatrice.  In southwest Iowa the heavier totals included; 7.5 inches in Glenwood, and around 5 inches at Harlan, Clarinda and Little Sioux.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values dropped to around 20 to 25 below zero as temperatures fell from the low single digits above and then below zero and northwest winds gusted between 20 and 35 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level trough of low pressure moved from the Rockies east toward the central U.S. on Saturday April 9th.  As it did, it caused a warm front to lift into eastern Nebraska and western Iowa.  Temperatures south of the front were in the 80s with dew point temperatures in the 60s.  Meanwhile, readings north of the boundary were in the 50s and 60s.  Moderate instability south of the front combined with high levels of wind shear allowed for supercell development over eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa during the late afternoon and early evening of April 9th.  A few of the storms that were near the front turned tornadic while the other supercells were prolific hail producers, in some cases producing very large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The second supercell thunderstorm that moved across Madison county during the evening produced hail up to baseball-size in the Norfolk area.  The hail caused considerable vehicle damage in town along with roof and siding damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure tracked from central Kansas into southern Missouri before weakening as it lifted northeast.  This system brought areas of heavy snow to northeast Nebraska with the heaviest from around Neligh northeast into Cedar and Knox counties.  In addition, several inches of accumulating snow fell from Wayne and Norfolk south to northern Butler county and some snow was also reported as far south as Lincoln and Fairbury.  In addition, the system brought widespread precipitation amounts of 0.75 to 1.75 inches to eastern Nebraska.  Strong easterly winds in advance of the system were replaced by north or northwest winds of 30 to 45 mph.  This added to travel woes in northeast Nebraska as snow rates were heavy enough to drop visibilities at times to less than 1/2 mile and cause accumulations on roads despite relatively mild ground temperatures and surface temperatures of 32 to 34 degrees during the snowfall.  Heavier totals included around 8 inches in Bloomfield and Coleridge, 7.5 inches in Neligh and around 6 inches in Oakdale and Hartington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 7.5 inches was reported in Neligh with similar totals likely across locations north and northeast of there.  The heavy wet snow was accompanied by northwest winds of 30 to 45 mph and occasionally dropped visibilities below 1/2 mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance that dropped southeast across Nebraska and Kansas brought light to occasionally moderate snow to much of eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa on Jan. 19th and early on the 20th.  Moisture with the storm was modest, but the storm was very efficient in producing snow as temperatures were sufficiently cold to bring high snow to liquid ratios, in some cases 20 to 1 or higher.  The heaviest snow from this system stretched from the Sandhills of Nebraska into southeast Nebraska and adjacent parts of Kansas and Missouri.  In southeast Nebraska amounts were generally in the 5 to 7 inch range southwest through southeast of Lincoln.  Heavier amounts in this area included; 7 inches near Salem, and around 6 inches in or near Tecumseh, Rulo, Friend, Wilber, Virginia and Auburn.  Winds were generally 10 to 15 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 12 to 15 hour period of light to occasionally moderate snow produced around 6 inches of snow over much of Saline county, including around 6 inches measured near Wilber and Friend.

Total snowfall from the storm was around 4 to 6 inches based on surrounding observations, although 3 inches recorded in Fremont was the highest reported in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level system dropped southeast from the northern Rockies and then split into two pieces, one tracked across the central plains while the other drifted southwest across the southwestern U.S.  Surface low pressure associated with the first piece tracked from northeast Texas across southeast Missouri and then into the lower Great Lakes region.  Arctic air was pulled in behind this system and brought a massive winter storm to a very large part of the U.S.  Freezing rain, heavy snow and blizzard conditions prevailed from the southern plains toward the Great Lakes, while record setting cold temperatures plunged southwest across western Texas, New Mexico and Arizona.||Across eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa the storm initially spread freezing drizzle or light freezing rain, mixed at times with light snow, across much of the area during the early morning hours of Jan. 31.  The precipitation changed over to all snow later on the 31st and continued into the late night hours of Feb. 1st.  As Arctic air spilled in behind the system, dangerously cold wind chill values spread over the region on the 1st and early on the 2nd.   Increasing north to northwest winds on the 1st caused considerable blowing and drifting snow which closed some county roads and created near-blizzard conditions.  The heavier snow from the storm was generally from around Lincoln and David City east and southeast into western Iowa, with most amounts across this area in the 5 to 7 inch range.  Heavier snow totals from the storm in eastern Nebraska included; 6.9 inches in Omaha, 6.6 inches at the NWS in Valley, 6.2 inches in Virginia, and around 6 inches in Rulo, Shubert, Auburn, near Cedar Bluffs and Papillion and around 5 inches at David City, Lincoln and Beatrice.  In southwest Iowa the heavier totals included; 7.5 inches in Glenwood, and around 5 inches at Harlan, Clarinda and Little Sioux.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values dropped to around 20 to 25 below zero as temperatures fell from the low single digits above and then below zero and northwest winds gusted between 20 and 35 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance that dropped southeast across Nebraska and Kansas brought light to occasionally moderate snow to much of eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa on Jan. 19th and early on the 20th.  Moisture with the storm was modest, but the storm was very efficient in producing snow as temperatures were sufficiently cold to bring high snow to liquid ratios, in some cases 20 to 1 or higher.  The heaviest snow from this system stretched from the Sandhills of Nebraska into southeast Nebraska and adjacent parts of Kansas and Missouri.  In southeast Nebraska amounts were generally in the 5 to 7 inch range southwest through southeast of Lincoln.  Heavier amounts in this area included; 7 inches near Salem, and around 6 inches in or near Tecumseh, Rulo, Friend, Wilber, Virginia and Auburn.  Winds were generally 10 to 15 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 12 to 15 hour period of light to occasionally moderate snow produced around 5 to 6 inches of snow over Johnson county.  Around 6 inches was reported near Tecumseh for one of the higher amounts received in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense, slow-moving, winter-like storm moved across the Central High Plains on April 14-15. Many residents were without power for periods of time ranging from 12 to 48 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Elsewhere across Dundy and Hitchcock counties, frequent wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful spring storm produced a wide variety of extreme weather, including severe thunderstorms across southwest Nebraska, high wind with gusts to near 60 mph, and moderate to heavy snow with blizzard conditions. Several reports of marginally severe hail occurred with fast-moving thunderstorms during the early afternoon hours across southwest Nebraska.

A powerful spring storm produced a wide variety of extreme weather, including severe thunderstorms across southwest Nebraska, high wind with gusts to near 60 mph, and moderate to heavy snow with blizzard conditions. Several reports of marginally severe hail occurred with fast-moving thunderstorms during the early afternoon hours across southwest Nebraska.

A combination of melting snow and warmer temperatures helped form ice jams along sections of eastern Nebraska rivers and streams over the last few weeks of February.  Most the flooding was noted along the Platte River from around Fremont downstream toward the confluence with the Elkhorn River and along the Elkhorn River from around Stanton toward Scribner.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the Platte River due to an ice jam when water briefly topped a Levee in the Willow Point area.  Little or no damage was reported in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of melting snow and warmer temperatures helped form ice jams along sections of eastern Nebraska rivers and streams over the last few weeks of February.  Most the flooding was noted along the Platte River from around Fremont downstream toward the confluence with the Elkhorn River and along the Elkhorn River from around Stanton toward Scribner.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice jam flooding was observed along the Elkhorn River from around the West Point area downstream to around Crowell.  Although the flooding was confined mostly to agricultural lowlands along the river, the flood waters did cross Highway 275 near Crowell prompting its closure for around a week.  The main ice jam was 1 mile upstream to 2 miles downstream of the Highway 32 bridge near West Point.  The jam caused the Elkhorn River at West Point to reach a crest a little over 14 feet on Feb. 22nd, flood stage is 12 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level system dropped southeast from the northern Rockies and then split into two pieces, one tracked across the central plains while the other drifted southwest across the southwestern U.S.  Surface low pressure associated with the first piece tracked from northeast Texas across southeast Missouri and then into the lower Great Lakes region.  Arctic air was pulled in behind this system and brought a massive winter storm to a very large part of the U.S.  Freezing rain, heavy snow and blizzard conditions prevailed from the southern plains toward the Great Lakes, while record setting cold temperatures plunged southwest across western Texas, New Mexico and Arizona.||Across eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa the storm initially spread freezing drizzle or light freezing rain, mixed at times with light snow, across much of the area during the early morning hours of Jan. 31.  The precipitation changed over to all snow later on the 31st and continued into the late night hours of Feb. 1st.  As Arctic air spilled in behind the system, dangerously cold wind chill values spread over the region on the 1st and early on the 2nd.   Increasing north to northwest winds on the 1st caused considerable blowing and drifting snow which closed some county roads and created near-blizzard conditions.  The heavier snow from the storm was generally from around Lincoln and David City east and southeast into western Iowa, with most amounts across this area in the 5 to 7 inch range.  Heavier snow totals from the storm in eastern Nebraska included; 6.9 inches in Omaha, 6.6 inches at the NWS in Valley, 6.2 inches in Virginia, and around 6 inches in Rulo, Shubert, Auburn, near Cedar Bluffs and Papillion and around 5 inches at David City, Lincoln and Beatrice.  In southwest Iowa the heavier totals included; 7.5 inches in Glenwood, and around 5 inches at Harlan, Clarinda and Little Sioux.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values dropped to around 20 below zero as temperatures fell to around zero and northwest winds gusted between 15 and 25 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pacific moisture and warm air wrapped around a large upper level low pressure system that moved from the northern Rockies slowly southeast into the eastern Dakotas from Saturday January 8 into the morning of January 11th.  This brought a 36 to 48 hour period of snow to eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa.  Although the snow was mostly light, periods of moderate snow were observed.  In addition, since the snow fell for an extended time, total amounts over most of eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa reached 7 to 10+ inches.  Most of the heavier amounts, centered on a 24 hour period from the morning of the 9th through the morning of the 10th, were in northeast Nebraska.  Winds during much of the event were 10 to 20 mph which limited blowing and drifting.  However, winds did pick up during the afternoon and evening of 1/10 and into the early morning hours of 1/11.  This caused drifting snow across most of the region with some reduced visibilities in blowing snow as well.  The stronger winds, and temperatures that dropped to zero or below, caused wind chill values to fall to 20 below to 29 below zero over northeast Nebraska on 1/11 and early on 1/12.  Heavier storm total snowfall amounts included...12 inches in Columbus and Oakdale, Nebraska, 11 inches near Verdel, Loretto, and Bloomfield, Nebraska and Little Sioux, Iowa and 10 inches at Norfolk, Wayne, Ft. Calhoun, Gretna, David City, Lyons, Neligh, and Weston, Nebraska and Castana, Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were around zero or colder for much of the period from the early morning hours of 1/11 into the morning hours of 1/12.  When combined with northwest winds of around 10 to 20 mph, wind chill values were lowered to 20 below to 29 below zero for much of this time period.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding along the Elkhorn River due to an ice jam was noted south of Highway 24 between Stanton and Norfolk.  The flooding was confined mainly to agricultural lowlands and damage was apparently minimal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure tracked from central Kansas into southern Missouri before weakening as it lifted northeast.  This system brought areas of heavy snow to northeast Nebraska with the heaviest from around Neligh northeast into Cedar and Knox counties.  In addition, several inches of accumulating snow fell from Wayne and Norfolk south to northern Butler county and some snow was also reported as far south as Lincoln and Fairbury.  In addition, the system brought widespread precipitation amounts of 0.75 to 1.75 inches to eastern Nebraska.  Strong easterly winds in advance of the system were replaced by north or northwest winds of 30 to 45 mph.  This added to travel woes in northeast Nebraska as snow rates were heavy enough to drop visibilities at times to less than 1/2 mile and cause accumulations on roads despite relatively mild ground temperatures and surface temperatures of 32 to 34 degrees during the snowfall.  Heavier totals included around 8 inches in Bloomfield and Coleridge, 7.5 inches in Neligh and around 6 inches in Oakdale and Hartington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 inches was reported in Pierce with heavier totals likely north of there based on observations in Knox county and visible satellite imagery the next day.  The heavy wet snow was accompanied by northwest winds of 30 to 45 mph and occasionally dropped visibilities below 1/2 mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pacific moisture and warm air wrapped around a large upper level low pressure system that moved from the northern Rockies slowly southeast into the eastern Dakotas from Saturday January 8 into the morning of January 11th.  This brought a 36 to 48 hour period of snow to eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa.  Although the snow was mostly light, periods of moderate snow were observed.  In addition, since the snow fell for an extended time, total amounts over most of eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa reached 7 to 10+ inches.  Most of the heavier amounts, centered on a 24 hour period from the morning of the 9th through the morning of the 10th, were in northeast Nebraska.  Winds during much of the event were 10 to 20 mph which limited blowing and drifting.  However, winds did pick up during the afternoon and evening of 1/10 and into the early morning hours of 1/11.  This caused drifting snow across most of the region with some reduced visibilities in blowing snow as well.  The stronger winds, and temperatures that dropped to zero or below, caused wind chill values to fall to 20 below to 29 below zero over northeast Nebraska on 1/11 and early on 1/12.  Heavier storm total snowfall amounts included...12 inches in Columbus and Oakdale, Nebraska, 11 inches near Verdel, Loretto, and Bloomfield, Nebraska and Little Sioux, Iowa and 10 inches at Norfolk, Wayne, Ft. Calhoun, Gretna, David City, Lyons, Neligh, and Weston, Nebraska and Castana, Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of light to moderate snow fell over the county with snow totals around 6 inches.  Beatrice reported 6 inches for one of the highest amounts received.  The snow fell over a 36+ hour period.  Winds during much of the snow were relatively light, but they did pick up some toward the end of the event which caused some drifting and blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level system dropped southeast from the northern Rockies and then split into two pieces, one tracked across the central plains while the other drifted southwest across the southwestern U.S.  Surface low pressure associated with the first piece tracked from northeast Texas across southeast Missouri and then into the lower Great Lakes region.  Arctic air was pulled in behind this system and brought a massive winter storm to a very large part of the U.S.  Freezing rain, heavy snow and blizzard conditions prevailed from the southern plains toward the Great Lakes, while record setting cold temperatures plunged southwest across western Texas, New Mexico and Arizona.||Across eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa the storm initially spread freezing drizzle or light freezing rain, mixed at times with light snow, across much of the area during the early morning hours of Jan. 31.  The precipitation changed over to all snow later on the 31st and continued into the late night hours of Feb. 1st.  As Arctic air spilled in behind the system, dangerously cold wind chill values spread over the region on the 1st and early on the 2nd.   Increasing north to northwest winds on the 1st caused considerable blowing and drifting snow which closed some county roads and created near-blizzard conditions.  The heavier snow from the storm was generally from around Lincoln and David City east and southeast into western Iowa, with most amounts across this area in the 5 to 7 inch range.  Heavier snow totals from the storm in eastern Nebraska included; 6.9 inches in Omaha, 6.6 inches at the NWS in Valley, 6.2 inches in Virginia, and around 6 inches in Rulo, Shubert, Auburn, near Cedar Bluffs and Papillion and around 5 inches at David City, Lincoln and Beatrice.  In southwest Iowa the heavier totals included; 7.5 inches in Glenwood, and around 5 inches at Harlan, Clarinda and Little Sioux.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Patchy freezing drizzle and light snow moved into the area during the early morning hours of 1/31 which caused very slick roads and numerous accidents during the morning commute in the Omaha area that day.  The precipitation changed to all snow later on the 31st and snow, occasionally moderate, continued through the late evening of Feb. 1.  Due to falling temperatures on the 1st, roads in the Omaha metro area were not treated in the afternoon which created very slick conditions during that evening's commute.  This in turn caused numerous accidents and traffic jams and made for a difficult commute.  North winds increased to 25 to 35 mph with gusts of 40 mph during the afternoon and evening of 2/1, which caused considerable blowing and drifting snow which closed some county roads and created near-blizzard conditions.  Total snowfall from the storm was around 5 or 6 inches with 5.5 inches recorded in Papillion and 5 inches near Gretna the higher amounts reported in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance that dropped southeast across Nebraska and Kansas brought light to occasionally moderate snow to much of eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa on Jan. 19th and early on the 20th.  Moisture with the storm was modest, but the storm was very efficient in producing snow as temperatures were sufficiently cold to bring high snow to liquid ratios, in some cases 20 to 1 or higher.  The heaviest snow from this system stretched from the Sandhills of Nebraska into southeast Nebraska and adjacent parts of Kansas and Missouri.  In southeast Nebraska amounts were generally in the 5 to 7 inch range southwest through southeast of Lincoln.  Heavier amounts in this area included; 7 inches near Salem, and around 6 inches in or near Tecumseh, Rulo, Friend, Wilber, Virginia and Auburn.  Winds were generally 10 to 15 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 12 to 15 hour period of light to occasionally moderate snow produced around 5 to 6 inches of snow over Pawnee county based on measurements from surrounding counties.

Arctic air was pulled in behind this system and brought a massive winter storm to a very large part of the U.S.  Freezing rain, heavy snow and blizzard conditions prevailed from the southern plains toward the Great Lakes, while record setting cold temperatures plunged southwest across western Texas, New Mexico and Arizona.||Across eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa the storm initially spread freezing drizzle or light freezing rain, mixed at times with light snow, across much of the area during the early morning hours of Jan. 31.  The precipitation changed over to all snow later on the 31st and continued into the late night hours of Feb. 1st.  As Arctic air spilled in behind the system, dangerously cold wind chill values spread over the region on the 1st and early on the 2nd.   Increasing north to northwest winds on the 1st caused considerable blowing and drifting snow which closed some county roads and created near-blizzard conditions.  The heavier snow from the storm was generally from around Lincoln and David City east and southeast into western Iowa, with most amounts across this area in the 5 to 7 inch range.  Heavier snow totals from the storm in eastern Nebraska included; 6.9 inches in Omaha, 6.6 inches at the NWS in Valley, 6.2 inches in Virginia, and around 6 inches in Rulo, Shubert, Auburn, near Cedar Bluffs and Papillion and around 5 inches at David City, Lincoln and Beatrice.  In southwest Iowa the heavier totals included; 7.5 inches in Glenwood, and around 5 inches at Harlan, Clarinda and Little Sioux.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although the snow/winter storm had ended over the area by this time, wind chill values were around 20 to 25 below zero.  These cold values then persisted into the late morning of 2/2.  The combination of temperatures falling from the low single digits above and then below zero and northwest winds gusting between 20 and 35 mph created these dangerously cold wind chill values.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pacific moisture and warm air wrapped around a large upper level low pressure system that moved from the northern Rockies slowly southeast into the eastern Dakotas from Saturday January 8 into the morning of January 11th.  This brought a 36 to 48 hour period of snow to eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa.  Although the snow was mostly light, periods of moderate snow were observed.  In addition, since the snow fell for an extended time, total amounts over most of eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa reached 7 to 10+ inches.  Most of the heavier amounts, centered on a 24 hour period from the morning of the 9th through the morning of the 10th, were in northeast Nebraska.  Winds during much of the event were 10 to 20 mph which limited blowing and drifting.  However, winds did pick up during the afternoon and evening of 1/10 and into the early morning hours of 1/11.  This caused drifting snow across most of the region with some reduced visibilities in blowing snow as well.  The stronger winds, and temperatures that dropped to zero or below, caused wind chill values to fall to 20 below to 29 below zero over northeast Nebraska on 1/11 and early on 1/12.  Heavier storm total snowfall amounts included...12 inches in Columbus and Oakdale, Nebraska, 11 inches near Verdel, Loretto, and Bloomfield, Nebraska and Little Sioux, Iowa and 10 inches at Norfolk, Wayne, Ft. Calhoun, Gretna, David City, Lyons, Neligh, and Weston, Nebraska and Castana, Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of light to moderate snow fell over the county with snow totals based on reports from surrounding counties likely between 6 and 8 inches.  The snow fell over a 36+ hour period and caused some local schools to close on the 10th and 11th.  Winds during much of the snow were relatively light, but they did pick up some toward the end of the event which caused some drifting and blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm tracking from eastern Colorado into northern Kansas and southern Nebraska brought areas of heavy snowfall to portions of southwest Nebraska on December 3rd, 2011. Storm total snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 12 inches across Frontier County. North winds of 20 to 30 mph also caused considerable blowing snow with drifts up to 4 feet across the southeastern part of the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative observer in Stockville reported 6 inches of snowfall with a storm total snowfall of 8 inches. Snowfall amounts across Frontier County ranged from 5 to 12 inches. Northerly winds of 20 to 30 mph caused considerable blowing and drifting snow during the morning and afternoon hours, especially across the southeastern part of the county. The blowing and drifting of the snow resulted in snow drifts up to 4 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For most of South Central Nebraska, this Wednesday featured the second-heaviest single snow event of the winter season, with most counties picking up between 3-6 inches in a 12-18 hour period. On the higher side of things though, a narrow northwest to southeast corridor running through the heart of the area, from near Loup City and Ord, through Hastings and Geneva tallied 6 to locally 8 inches. In contrast, lesser amounts of 1-3 inches targeted locations generally southwest of a line from Elwood to Franklin, although some light freezing drizzle did affect this area as well. The heaviest snow fell during the late morning and early afternoon hours, then tapered off through the evening. Fortunately, winds were not overly strong during this snow event. However,  a number of accidents were reported around the area, including a chain reaction accident involving six cars in Howard County, and a low-speed semi-school bus crash near Ravenna, neither of which resulted in any injuries.  A two-car accident in Hamilton County did result in three injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations ranged from 7 to 9 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system tracked northeast across western and north central Nebraska during the overnight hours on February 19th through the morning hours on February 20th.  Warm air lifting over subfreezing temperatures near the surface caused scattered thunderstorms with freezing rain and sleet to occur.  Significant ice accumulations of a quarter to half inch occurred across the eastern half of north central Nebraska, while significant accumulations of sleet and freezing rain occurred across western portions of north central Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative observer located in Bassett reported a quarter inch of ice accumulation from freezing rain.  Ice accumulations around a quarter inch were common across Rock county. Light sleet accumulations around a quarter inch also occurred as the system exited during the morning of February 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a brief warm spell in late January, winter quickly returned to South Central Nebraska on the last day of the month as a large upper level low pressure system carved into the central United States, bringing some of the coldest air of the season with it. On Monday the 31st, the day started out with light freezing drizzle in parts of the area, but mainly for counties near the Kansas border. As the day wore on into the evening, light snow gradually overspread the area from northwest to southeast, with accumulations of 1-3 inches by midnight in some locations. However, the bigger story became increasing north winds, with sustained speeds to around 30 MPH and gusts to over 40 MPH resulting in at least minor drifting and visibility restrictions to less than one half mile. In addition, with temperatures plunging to a few degrees either side of zero, the strong winds ushered in the coldest wind chill temperatures of the season, with places such as Ord reporting a wind chill of 31 degrees below zero just before midnight. The combination of light snow, strong winds and dangerous wind chills continued into February 1st, when near white-out conditions occurred in some areas along and east of the Highway 281 corridor, including around Hastings. Wind chills on the morning of the 1st were commonly around -30 across much of South Central Nebraska. By the time snow ended for good on the 1st, storm total accumulations of 1-3 inches were reported west of the Highway 281 corridor, and 2-4 inches to the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall accumulations of 1 to 3 inches, combined with strong winds of 25 to 30 mph and gusts approaching 45 mph at times, resulted in reduced visibilities and hazardous travel across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level system that dropped southeast from the northern Rockies split into two pieces, one tracked across the central plains while the other drifted southwest across the southwestern U.S.  Surface low pressure associated with the first piece tracked from northeast Texas across southeast Missouri and then into the lower Great Lakes region.  Arctic air was pulled in behind the lead system and brought a massive winter storm to a very large part of the U.S.  Freezing rain, heavy snow and blizzard conditions prevailed from the southern plains toward the Great Lakes, while record setting cold temperatures plunged southwest across western Texas, New Mexico and Arizona behind the southwest digging system.||Across eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa the storm initially spread freezing drizzle or light freezing rain, mixed at times with light snow, across much of the area during the early morning hours of Jan. 31.  The precipitation changed over to all snow later on the 31st and continued into the late night hours of Feb. 1st.  As Arctic air spilled in behind the system, dangerously cold wind chill values spread over the region late on the 1st and early on the 2nd.   Increasing north to northwest winds on the 1st caused considerable blowing and drifting snow which closed some county roads and created near-blizzard conditions.  The heavier snow from the storm was generally from around Lincoln and David City east and southeast into western Iowa, with most amounts across this area in the 5 to 7 inch range.  Heavier snow totals from the storm in eastern Nebraska included; 6.9 inches in Omaha, 6.6 inches at the NWS in Valley, 6.2 inches in Virginia, and around 6 inches in Rulo, Shubert, Auburn, near Cedar Bluffs and Papillion and around 5 inches at David City, Lincoln and Beatrice.  In southwest Iowa the heavier totals included; 7.5 inches in Glenwood, and around 5 inches at Harlan, Clarinda and Little Sioux.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Patchy freezing drizzle and light snow moved into the area during the early morning hours of 1/31.  The precipitation changed to all snow later on the 31st and snow, occasionally moderate, continued through the afternoon of Feb. 1.  North winds increased to 25 to 35 mph with gusts of 40 mph during the afternoon and evening of 2/1, which caused considerable blowing and drifting snow which closed some county roads and created near-blizzard conditions.  Although total snowfall from the storm was only 2 to 4 inches, the poor conditions made for difficult traveling and closed many area schools.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance dropped southeast across Nebraska during the afternoon and evening of Jan. 22.  This caused low pressure to develop over eastern Kansas with an inverted surface trough extending north into eastern Nebraska.  Snow, occasionally heavy, developed along this trough during the afternoon of the 22nd and continued into the early morning hours of Jan. 23rd.  Snow intensities in some cases exceeded 1 inch per hour, especially in a relatively narrow band from Norfolk through Fremont and into the western sections of Omaha where storm totals reached 6 to 8 inches or more.   North to northeast winds increased to 15 to 25 mph toward the end of the event further reducing visibilities in blowing snow.  Cold high pressure building in behind the system dropped lows by the 23rd to as cold as 15 below to 20 below zero in northeast Nebraska with wind chill values of 20 to 29 below zero.  Heavier storm totals from the storm included...8.5 inches in Fremont, around 7 inches at Norfolk, Oakdale, the NWS in Valley, Schuyler and Bennington Nebraska and Sidney Iowa.  There were also reports of 7 to 9 inches across the western sections of Omaha.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall over much of Dodge county from this storm was between 6 and 8 inches with 8.5 inches measured in Fremont for one of the higher amounts reported in the county.  The snow was especially heavy during the late afternoon and early evening hours of 1/22.  North winds increased to 15 to 25 mph toward the end of the event producing a period of poor visibilities in blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For most of South Central Nebraska, this Wednesday featured the second-heaviest single snow event of the winter season, with most counties picking up between 3-6 inches in a 12-18 hour period. On the higher side of things though, a narrow northwest to southeast corridor running through the heart of the area, from near Loup City and Ord, through Hastings and Geneva tallied 6 to locally 8 inches. In contrast, lesser amounts of 1-3 inches targeted locations generally southwest of a line from Elwood to Franklin, although some light freezing drizzle did affect this area as well. The heaviest snow fell during the late morning and early afternoon hours, then tapered off through the evening. Fortunately, winds were not overly strong during this snow event. However,  a number of accidents were reported around the area, including a chain reaction accident involving six cars in Howard County, and a low-speed semi-school bus crash near Ravenna, neither of which resulted in any injuries.  A two-car accident in Hamilton County did result in three injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations ranged from 5 to 6 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms, some severe, developed near the Kansas and Nebraska border south of Beatrice during the early afternoon of May 11th.  Temperatures in the 80s and dew point temperatures in the mid/upper 60s from southeast Nebraska into easterly Kansas created moderately unstable conditions as a large upper level low pressure system organized over Colorado.  Due to the circulation of the upper system, the storms lifted north across eastern Nebraska and produced areas of damaging winds and several large hail reports.  With a boundary bisecting east central Nebraska and western Iowa a few tornadoes also spun-up just west and east of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Home weather equipment measured a thunderstorm wind gust of 78 mph a few miles northeast of Plymouth.  The winds knocked down a few tree branches in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system tracked northeast across western and north central Nebraska during the overnight hours on February 19th through the morning hours on February 20th.  Warm air lifting over subfreezing temperatures near the surface caused scattered thunderstorms with freezing rain and sleet to occur.  Significant ice accumulations of a quarter to half inch occurred across the eastern half of north central Nebraska, while significant accumulations of sleet and freezing rain occurred across western portions of north central Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative observer located in Atkinson reported one inch of ice accumulation from freezing rain.  Ice accumulations around a quarter to half inch were common across Holt county. Light sleet accumulations from a tenth to a quarter inch also occurred as the system exited during the morning of February 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance dropped southeast across Nebraska during the afternoon and evening of Jan. 22.  This caused low pressure to develop over eastern Kansas with an inverted surface trough extending north into eastern Nebraska.  Snow, occasionally heavy, developed along this trough during the afternoon of the 22nd and continued into the early morning hours of Jan. 23rd.  Snow intensities in some cases exceeded 1 inch per hour, especially in a relatively narrow band from Norfolk through Fremont and into the western sections of Omaha where storm totals reached 6 to 8 inches or more.   North to northeast winds increased to 15 to 25 mph toward the end of the event further reducing visibilities in blowing snow.  Cold high pressure building in behind the system dropped lows by the 23rd to as cold as 15 below to 20 below zero in northeast Nebraska with wind chill values of 20 to 29 below zero.  Heavier storm totals from the storm included...8.5 inches in Fremont, around 7 inches at Norfolk, Oakdale, the NWS in Valley, Schuyler and Bennington Nebraska and Sidney Iowa.  There were also reports of 7 to 9 inches across the western sections of Omaha.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall over much Sarpy county was mostly 6 to 8 inches with 7.5 inches measured near Gretna for one of the higher amounts reported in the county.  The snow was especially heavy during the late afternoon and evening hours of 1/22.  North winds increased to 15 to 25 mph toward the end of the event producing a period of poor visibilities in blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow produced heavy accumulations of 8 to 10 inches in an area near the Missouri River in southeast South Dakota during a 24 hour period beginning in the late afternoon of January 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 8 to 10 inches, including 10 inches in the far southwest corner of the county near Emerson, and 10 inches also at South Sioux City. The snowfall was accompanied by light to moderate winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a brief warm spell in late January, winter quickly returned to South Central Nebraska on the last day of the month as a large upper level low pressure system carved into the central United States, bringing some of the coldest air of the season with it. However, the bigger story became increasing north winds, with sustained speeds to around 30 MPH and gusts to over 40 MPH resulting in at least minor drifting and visibility restrictions to less than one half mile. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall accumulations of 1 to 3 inches, combined with strong winds of 25 to 30 mph and gusts approaching 45 mph at times, resulted in reduced visibilities and hazardous travel across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level system that dropped southeast from the northern Rockies split into two pieces, one tracked across the central plains while the other drifted southwest across the southwestern U.S.  Surface low pressure associated with the first piece tracked from northeast Texas across southeast Missouri and then into the lower Great Lakes region.  Arctic air was pulled in behind the lead system and brought a massive winter storm to a very large part of the U.S.  Freezing rain, heavy snow and blizzard conditions prevailed from the southern plains toward the Great Lakes, while record setting cold temperatures plunged southwest across western Texas, New Mexico and Arizona behind the southwest digging system.||Across eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa the storm initially spread freezing drizzle or light freezing rain, mixed at times with light snow, across much of the area during the early morning hours of Jan. 31.  The precipitation changed over to all snow later on the 31st and continued into the late night hours of Feb. 1st.  As Arctic air spilled in behind the system, dangerously cold wind chill values spread over the region late on the 1st and early on the 2nd.   Increasing north to northwest winds on the 1st caused considerable blowing and drifting snow which closed some county roads and created near-blizzard conditions.  The heavier snow from the storm was generally from around Lincoln and David City east and southeast into western Iowa, with most amounts across this area in the 5 to 7 inch range.  Heavier snow totals from the storm in eastern Nebraska included; 6.9 inches in Omaha, 6.6 inches at the NWS in Valley, 6.2 inches in Virginia, and around 6 inches in Rulo, Shubert, Auburn, near Cedar Bluffs and Papillion and around 5 inches at David City, Lincoln and Beatrice.  In southwest Iowa the heavier totals included; 7.5 inches in Glenwood, and around 5 inches at Harlan, Clarinda and Little Sioux.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Patchy freezing drizzle and light snow moved into the area during the early morning hours of 1/31.  The precipitation changed to all snow later on the 31st and snow, occasionally moderate, continued through the late evening of Feb. 1.  North winds increased to 25 to 35 mph with gusts of 40 mph during the afternoon and evening of 2/1, which caused considerable blowing and drifting snow which closed some county roads and created near-blizzard conditions.  Total snowfall from the storm was around 6 inches based on surrounding observations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For most of South Central Nebraska, the first widespread heavy snow of the season fell between Saturday the 8th and Monday the 10th as an expansive upper level low pressure system centered over the Northern Rockies built increasingly farther into the Central Plains. As it turned out, this ended up being the most significant snow event of the 2010-2011 winter season for nearly the entire area, with most counties reporting between 7-12 inches, and some of the highest totals in this range targeting places such as Hastings, Grand Island and Ord. For Grand Island and Hastings, this event set new records for January 9th calendar day snowfall and and liquid equivalent precipitation. This was a prolonged snow event, with many areas experiencing more than one round over a 36 to 48-hour period. The first round of fairly light accumulations moved in on the afternoon of the 8th into the morning hours on the 9th, with most places reporting no more than 2-4 inches by mid-day on the 9th.  Things really picked up during the mid-afternoon hours on the 9th, and lasting through the overnight hours into the 10th. During this time, a nearly stationary area of light to moderate, and occasionally heavy snow fell across nearly all of South Central Nebraska. On the afternoon of the 9th, a stretch of Interstate 80 between Gothenburg and Cozad was closed due to several accidents. By mid-morning on Monday the 10th, light snow or flurries still lingered on across the entire area, but the vast majority of accumulating snow was over.||Fortunately, winds were rather light during the period of heaviest snowfall from the 9th into the morning hours on the 10th, with limited impact from blowing and drifting. However, northwesterly winds picked up during the day on the 10th, with gusts of 20-30 mph resulting in minor drifting and locally reduced visibilities. Once the snow ended, bitterly cold air became the story for a few days, with most of the area struggling to reach 10 degrees on both the 11th and 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations ranged from 10 to 12 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system tracked northeast across western and north central Nebraska during the overnight hours on February 19th through the morning hours on February 20th.  Warm air lifting over subfreezing temperatures near the surface caused scattered thunderstorms with freezing rain and sleet to occur.  Significant ice accumulations of a quarter to half inch occurred across the eastern half of north central Nebraska, while significant accumulations of sleet and freezing rain occurred across western portions of north central Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported around a quarter inch of ice accumulation from freezing rain located 11 miles southeast of Springview.  Ice accumulations around a quarter inch were common across Keya Paha county. Light snow and sleet accumulations around an inch also occurred as the system exited during the morning of February 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system tracked northeast across western and north central Nebraska during the overnight hours on February 19th through the morning hours on February 20th.  Warm air lifting over subfreezing temperatures near the surface caused scattered thunderstorms with freezing rain and sleet to occur.  Significant ice accumulations of a quarter to half inch occurred across the eastern half of north central Nebraska, while significant accumulations of sleet and freezing rain occurred across western portions of north central Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative observer in Valentine reported 0.70 inch of sleet.  Sleet accumulations of a half inch or more were common across eastern Cherry county. Ice accumulations from freezing rain of a quarter inch or less were also reported.  Snowfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches occurred as the system exited during the morning hours on February 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms, some severe, developed near the Kansas and Nebraska border south of Beatrice during the early afternoon of May 11th.  Temperatures in the 80s and dew point temperatures in the mid/upper 60s from southeast Nebraska into easterly Kansas created moderately unstable conditions as a large upper level low pressure system organized over Colorado.  Due to the circulation of the upper system, the storms lifted north across eastern Nebraska and produced areas of damaging winds and several large hail reports.  With a boundary bisecting east central Nebraska and western Iowa a few tornadoes also spun-up just west and east of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail lasted several minutes and just followed thunderstorm wind gusts that were estimated at around 70 mph.

By mid-morning on Monday the 10th, light snow or flurries still lingered on across the entire area, but the vast majority of accumulating snow was over.||Fortunately, winds were rather light during the period of heaviest snowfall from the 9th into the morning hours on the 10th, with limited impact from blowing and drifting. However, northwesterly winds picked up during the day on the 10th, with gusts of 20-30 mph resulting in minor drifting and locally reduced visibilities. Once the snow ended, bitterly cold air became the story for a few days, with most of the area struggling to reach 10 degrees on both the 11th and 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations ranged from 9 to 11 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a brief warm spell in late January, winter quickly returned to South Central Nebraska on the last day of the month as a large upper level low pressure system carved into the central United States, bringing some of the coldest air of the season with it. On Monday the 31st, the day started out with light freezing drizzle in parts of the area, but mainly for counties near the Kansas border. As the day wore on into the evening, light snow gradually overspread the area from northwest to southeast, with accumulations of 1-3 inches by midnight in some locations. However, the bigger story became increasing north winds, with sustained speeds to around 30 MPH and gusts to over 40 MPH resulting in at least minor drifting and visibility restrictions to less than one half mile. In addition, with temperatures plunging to a few degrees either side of zero, the strong winds ushered in the coldest wind chill temperatures of the season, with places such as Ord reporting a wind chill of 31 degrees below zero just before midnight. The combination of light snow, strong winds and dangerous wind chills continued into February 1st, when near white-out conditions occurred in some areas along and east of the Highway 281 corridor, including around Hastings. Wind chills on the morning of the 1st were commonly around -30 across much of South Central Nebraska. By the time snow ended for good on the 1st, storm total accumulations of 1-3 inches were reported west of the Highway 281 corridor, and 2-4 inches to the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall accumulations of 2 to 4 inches combined with strong winds of 25 to 30 mph and periodic gusts approaching 45 mph brought reduced visibilities, occasional white out conditions and hazardous travel to the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For most of South Central Nebraska, this Wednesday featured the second-heaviest single snow event of the winter season, with most counties picking up between 3-6 inches in a 12-18 hour period. On the higher side of things though, a narrow northwest to southeast corridor running through the heart of the area, from near Loup City and Ord, through Hastings and Geneva tallied 6 to locally 8 inches. In contrast, lesser amounts of 1-3 inches targeted locations generally southwest of a line from Elwood to Franklin, although some light freezing drizzle did affect this area as well. The heaviest snow fell during the late morning and early afternoon hours, then tapered off through the evening. Fortunately, winds were not overly strong during this snow event. However,  a number of accidents were reported around the area, including a chain reaction accident involving six cars in Howard County, and a low-speed semi-school bus crash near Ravenna, neither of which resulted in any injuries.  A two-car accident in Hamilton County did result in three injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations ranged from 2 to 4 inches across the county. Light freezing rain and freezing drizzle resulted in slick spots on area roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense upper level low pressure system that closed off over the Dakotas on April 29 and April 30 sent a cold front across eastern Nebraska during the evening of April 29.  Scattered thunderstorms developed along and behind the front, at least one of which produced wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 60 mph blew a large secured grain bin a few hundred yards off its foundation and damaged a few others around 3 miles southwest of Albion.  The winds also caused tree damage around that area northeast into Albion.  A trampoline in the area was also wrapped around a propane tank and swing set flipped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance dropped southeast across Nebraska during the afternoon and evening of Jan. 22.  This caused low pressure to develop over eastern Kansas with an inverted surface trough extending north into eastern Nebraska.  Snow, occasionally heavy, developed along this trough during the afternoon of the 22nd and continued into the early morning hours of Jan. 23rd.  Snow intensities in some cases exceeded 1 inch per hour, especially in a relatively narrow band from Norfolk through Fremont and into the western sections of Omaha where storm totals reached 6 to 8 inches or more.   North to northeast winds increased to 15 to 25 mph toward the end of the event further reducing visibilities in blowing snow.  Cold high pressure building in behind the system dropped lows by the 23rd to as cold as 15 below to 20 below zero in northeast Nebraska with wind chill values of 20 to 29 below zero.  Heavier storm totals from the storm included...8.5 inches in Fremont, around 7 inches at Norfolk, Oakdale, the NWS in Valley, Schuyler and Bennington Nebraska and Sidney Iowa.  There were also reports of 7 to 9 inches across the western sections of Omaha.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures dropped to 5 below to 10 below zero during the morning hours of 1/23.  When combined with north winds of around 10 mph, wind chill values were lowered to 20 below to 29 below zero for several hours early in the morning.  Although temperatures fell a little more around sunrise, winds became nearly calm.

Things really picked up during the mid-afternoon hours on the 9th, and lasting through the overnight hours into the 10th. During this time, a nearly stationary area of light to moderate, and occasionally heavy snow fell across nearly all of South Central Nebraska. On the afternoon of the 9th, a stretch of Interstate 80 between Gothenburg and Cozad was closed due to several accidents. By mid-morning on Monday the 10th, light snow or flurries still lingered on across the entire area, but the vast majority of accumulating snow was over.||Fortunately, winds were rather light during the period of heaviest snowfall from the 9th into the morning hours on the 10th, with limited impact from blowing and drifting. However, northwesterly winds picked up during the day on the 10th, with gusts of 20-30 mph resulting in minor drifting and locally reduced visibilities. Once the snow ended, bitterly cold air became the story for a few days, with most of the area struggling to reach 10 degrees on both the 11th and 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations ranged from 9 to 11 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level system that dropped southeast from the northern Rockies split into two pieces, one tracked across the central plains while the other drifted southwest across the southwestern U.S.  Surface low pressure associated with the first piece tracked from northeast Texas across southeast Missouri and then into the lower Great Lakes region.  Arctic air was pulled in behind the lead system and brought a massive winter storm to a very large part of the U.S.  Freezing rain, heavy snow and blizzard conditions prevailed from the southern plains toward the Great Lakes, while record setting cold temperatures plunged southwest across western Texas, New Mexico and Arizona behind the southwest digging system.||Across eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa the storm initially spread freezing drizzle or light freezing rain, mixed at times with light snow, across much of the area during the early morning hours of Jan. 31.  The precipitation changed over to all snow later on the 31st and continued into the late night hours of Feb. 1st.  As Arctic air spilled in behind the system, dangerously cold wind chill values spread over the region late on the 1st and early on the 2nd.   Increasing north to northwest winds on the 1st caused considerable blowing and drifting snow which closed some county roads and created near-blizzard conditions.  The heavier snow from the storm was generally from around Lincoln and David City east and southeast into western Iowa, with most amounts across this area in the 5 to 7 inch range.  Heavier snow totals from the storm in eastern Nebraska included; 6.9 inches in Omaha, 6.6 inches at the NWS in Valley, 6.2 inches in Virginia, and around 6 inches in Rulo, Shubert, Auburn, near Cedar Bluffs and Papillion and around 5 inches at David City, Lincoln and Beatrice.  In southwest Iowa the heavier totals included; 7.5 inches in Glenwood, and around 5 inches at Harlan, Clarinda and Little Sioux.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Patchy freezing drizzle and light snow moved into the area during the early morning hours of 1/31.  The precipitation changed to all snow later on the 31st and snow, occasionally moderate, continued through the late evening of Feb. 1.  North winds increased to 25 to 35 mph with gusts of 40 mph during the afternoon and evening of 2/1, which caused considerable blowing and drifting snow which closed some county roads and created near-blizzard conditions.  Total snowfall from the storm was around 6 inches with around 6 inches reported at both Shubert and Rulo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance dropped southeast across Nebraska during the afternoon and evening of Jan. 22.  This caused low pressure to develop over eastern Kansas with an inverted surface trough extending north into eastern Nebraska.  Snow, occasionally heavy, developed along this trough during the afternoon of the 22nd and continued into the early morning hours of Jan. 23rd.  Snow intensities in some cases exceeded 1 inch per hour, especially in a relatively narrow band from Norfolk through Fremont and into the western sections of Omaha where storm totals reached 6 to 8 inches or more.   North to northeast winds increased to 15 to 25 mph toward the end of the event further reducing visibilities in blowing snow.  Cold high pressure building in behind the system dropped lows by the 23rd to as cold as 15 below to 20 below zero in northeast Nebraska with wind chill values of 20 to 29 below zero.  Heavier storm totals from the storm included...8.5 inches in Fremont, around 7 inches at Norfolk, Oakdale, the NWS in Valley, Schuyler and Bennington Nebraska and Sidney Iowa.  There were also reports of 7 to 9 inches across the western sections of Omaha.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures dropped to 5 below to 10 below zero during the morning hours of 1/23.  When combined with north winds of around 10 mph, wind chill values were lowered to 20 below to 29 below zero for several hours early in the morning.  Although temperatures fell a little more around sunrise, winds became nearly calm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For most of South Central Nebraska, this Wednesday featured the second-heaviest single snow event of the winter season, with most counties picking up between 3-6 inches in a 12-18 hour period. On the higher side of things though, a narrow northwest to southeast corridor running through the heart of the area, from near Loup City and Ord, through Hastings and Geneva tallied 6 to locally 8 inches. In contrast, lesser amounts of 1-3 inches targeted locations generally southwest of a line from Elwood to Franklin, although some light freezing drizzle did affect this area as well. The heaviest snow fell during the late morning and early afternoon hours, then tapered off through the evening. Fortunately, winds were not overly strong during this snow event. However,  a number of accidents were reported around the area, including a chain reaction accident involving six cars in Howard County, and a low-speed semi-school bus crash near Ravenna, neither of which resulted in any injuries.  A two-car accident in Hamilton County did result in three injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations ranged from 4 to 5 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system tracked northeast across western and north central Nebraska during the overnight hours on February 19th through the morning hours on February 20th.  Warm air lifting over subfreezing temperatures near the surface caused scattered thunderstorms with freezing rain and sleet to occur.  Significant ice accumulations of a quarter to half inch occurred across the eastern half of north central Nebraska, while significant accumulations of sleet and freezing rain occurred across western portions of north central Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement in Mullen reported 0.85 inch of sleet.  Sleet accumulations of a half inch or more were common across Hooker county. Ice accumulations from freezing rain of a tenth of an inch were also reported.  Snowfall amounts around an inch also occurred as the system exited during the morning hours on February 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level system that dropped southeast from the northern Rockies split into two pieces, one tracked across the central plains while the other drifted southwest across the southwestern U.S.  Surface low pressure associated with the first piece tracked from northeast Texas across southeast Missouri and then into the lower Great Lakes region.  Arctic air was pulled in behind the lead system and brought a massive winter storm to a very large part of the U.S.  Freezing rain, heavy snow and blizzard conditions prevailed from the southern plains toward the Great Lakes, while record setting cold temperatures plunged southwest across western Texas, New Mexico and Arizona behind the southwest digging system.||Across eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa the storm initially spread freezing drizzle or light freezing rain, mixed at times with light snow, across much of the area during the early morning hours of Jan. 31.  The precipitation changed over to all snow later on the 31st and continued into the late night hours of Feb. 1st.  As Arctic air spilled in behind the system, dangerously cold wind chill values spread over the region late on the 1st and early on the 2nd.   Increasing north to northwest winds on the 1st caused considerable blowing and drifting snow which closed some county roads and created near-blizzard conditions.  The heavier snow from the storm was generally from around Lincoln and David City east and southeast into western Iowa, with most amounts across this area in the 5 to 7 inch range.  Heavier snow totals from the storm in eastern Nebraska included; 6.9 inches in Omaha, 6.6 inches at the NWS in Valley, 6.2 inches in Virginia, and around 6 inches in Rulo, Shubert, Auburn, near Cedar Bluffs and Papillion and around 5 inches at David City, Lincoln and Beatrice.  In southwest Iowa the heavier totals included; 7.5 inches in Glenwood, and around 5 inches at Harlan, Clarinda and Little Sioux.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Patchy freezing drizzle and light snow moved into the area during the early morning hours of 1/31.  The precipitation changed to all snow later on the 31st and snow, occasionally moderate, continued through the late evening of Feb. 1.  North winds increased to 25 to 35 mph with gusts of 40 mph during the afternoon and evening of 2/1, which caused considerable blowing and drifting snow which closed some county roads and created near-blizzard conditions.  Total snowfall from the storm was around 6 inches based on surrounding observations.

There were also reports of 7 to 9 inches across the western sections of Omaha.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures dropped to 5 below to 10 below zero during the morning hours of 1/23.  When combined with north winds of around 10 mph, wind chill values were lowered to 20 below to 29 below zero for several hours early in the morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following several weeks of Platte River ice jam flooding farther upstream toward Grand Island and Kearney, ice jamming issues made their presence known along the Merrick and Hamilton County line near Central City for a few days in mid-January between the 10th and 13th. The primary impact was felt approximately two miles south of Central City along Highway 14 in the Willowbend residential area, where water got within a few feet from an access road leading to homes.  One business south of Central City was flooded, and water rose up to the bottom of the Highway 14 bridge.

In a continuation from December, ice jam flooding remained an issue along portions of the Platte River mainly in early to mid-January, with the worst problems now focused across southern Buffalo and northern Kearney Counties. Some of the worst flooding that affected residences started on the 2nd along a portion of Sweetwater Avenue roughly three miles east of Kearney, and between the north channel of the river and the eastbound lanes of Interstate 80. In this area, several short term evacuations occurred. Other locations impacted by lowland flooding east of Kearney included a youth camp south of I-80. Water also plagued areas near the I-80 Minden interchange, where water approached the on-ramp for eastbound vehicles. During this time, a temporary river gauge was installed several miles downstream from Kearney area to help monitor rising water levels.

A strong storm system tracked northeast across western and north central Nebraska during the overnight hours on February 19th through the morning hours on February 20th.  Warm air lifting over subfreezing temperatures near the surface caused scattered thunderstorms with freezing rain and sleet to occur.  Significant ice accumulations of a quarter to half inch occurred across the eastern half of north central Nebraska, while significant accumulations of sleet and freezing rain occurred across western portions of north central Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: General public reported a quarter inch or ice in Dunning.  Ice accumulations around a quarter to half inch were common across Blaine county. Light sleet accumulations from a tenth to a quarter inch also occurred as the system exited during the morning of February 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a brief warm spell in late January, winter quickly returned to South Central Nebraska on the last day of the month as a large upper level low pressure system carved into the central United States, bringing some of the coldest air of the season with it. On Monday the 31st, the day started out with light freezing drizzle in parts of the area, but mainly for counties near the Kansas border. As the day wore on into the evening, light snow gradually overspread the area from northwest to southeast, with accumulations of 1-3 inches by midnight in some locations. However, the bigger story became increasing north winds, with sustained speeds to around 30 MPH and gusts to over 40 MPH resulting in at least minor drifting and visibility restrictions to less than one half mile. In addition, with temperatures plunging to a few degrees either side of zero, the strong winds ushered in the coldest wind chill temperatures of the season, with places such as Ord reporting a wind chill of 31 degrees below zero just before midnight. The combination of light snow, strong winds and dangerous wind chills continued into February 1st, when near white-out conditions occurred in some areas along and east of the Highway 281 corridor, including around Hastings. Wind chills on the morning of the 1st were commonly around -30 across much of South Central Nebraska. By the time snow ended for good on the 1st, storm total accumulations of 1-3 inches were reported west of the Highway 281 corridor, and 2-4 inches to the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall accumulations of 1 to 3 inches, combined with strong winds of 25 to 30 mph and gusts approaching 45 mph at times, resulted in reduced visibilities and hazardous travel across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level system that dropped southeast from the northern Rockies split into two pieces, one tracked across the central plains while the other drifted southwest across the southwestern U.S.  Surface low pressure associated with the first piece tracked from northeast Texas across southeast Missouri and then into the lower Great Lakes region.  Arctic air was pulled in behind the lead system and brought a massive winter storm to a very large part of the U.S.  Freezing rain, heavy snow and blizzard conditions prevailed from the southern plains toward the Great Lakes, while record setting cold temperatures plunged southwest across western Texas, New Mexico and Arizona behind the southwest digging system.||Across eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa the storm initially spread freezing drizzle or light freezing rain, mixed at times with light snow, across much of the area during the early morning hours of Jan. 31.  The precipitation changed over to all snow later on the 31st and continued into the late night hours of Feb. 1st.  As Arctic air spilled in behind the system, dangerously cold wind chill values spread over the region late on the 1st and early on the 2nd.   Increasing north to northwest winds on the 1st caused considerable blowing and drifting snow which closed some county roads and created near-blizzard conditions.  The heavier snow from the storm was generally from around Lincoln and David City east and southeast into western Iowa, with most amounts across this area in the 5 to 7 inch range.  Heavier snow totals from the storm in eastern Nebraska included; 6.9 inches in Omaha, 6.6 inches at the NWS in Valley, 6.2 inches in Virginia, and around 6 inches in Rulo, Shubert, Auburn, near Cedar Bluffs and Papillion and around 5 inches at David City, Lincoln and Beatrice.  In southwest Iowa the heavier totals included; 7.5 inches in Glenwood, and around 5 inches at Harlan, Clarinda and Little Sioux.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Patchy freezing drizzle and light snow moved into the area during the early morning hours of 1/31.  The precipitation changed to all snow later on the 31st and snow, occasionally moderate, continued through the late afternoon of Feb. 1.  North winds increased to 25 to 35 mph with gusts of 40 mph during the afternoon and evening of 2/1, which caused considerable blowing and drifting snow which closed some county roads and created near-blizzard conditions.  Although total snowfall from the storm was only 2 to 4 inches, the poor conditions made for difficult traveling and closed many area schools.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance dropped southeast across Nebraska during the afternoon and evening of Jan. 22.  This caused low pressure to develop over eastern Kansas with an inverted surface trough extending north into eastern Nebraska.  Snow, occasionally heavy, developed along this trough during the afternoon of the 22nd and continued into the early morning hours of Jan. 23rd.  Snow intensities in some cases exceeded 1 inch per hour, especially in a relatively narrow band from Norfolk through Fremont and into the western sections of Omaha where storm totals reached 6 to 8 inches or more.   North to northeast winds increased to 15 to 25 mph toward the end of the event further reducing visibilities in blowing snow.  Cold high pressure building in behind the system dropped lows by the 23rd to as cold as 15 below to 20 below zero in northeast Nebraska with wind chill values of 20 to 29 below zero.  Heavier storm totals from the storm included...8.5 inches in Fremont, around 7 inches at Norfolk, Oakdale, the NWS in Valley, Schuyler and Bennington Nebraska and Sidney Iowa.  There were also reports of 7 to 9 inches across the western sections of Omaha.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall over much of Platte county from this storm was around 6 inches, with 6 inches reported by the cooperative observer near Columbus.  The snow was especially heavy during the late afternoon and early evening hours of 1/22.  North winds increased to 15 to 25 mph toward the end of the event producing a period of poor visibilities in blowing snow.
